records.ety: [Prob. originally imp. & p. p. of wave, v. t. See Wave to waver.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Wafted; p. pr. & vb. n. Wafting.]

defs: To give notice to by waving something; to wave the hand to; to beckon.
quotes: But soft: who wafts us yonder?

defs: To cause to move or go in a wavy manner, or by the impulse of waves, as of water or air; to bear along on a buoyant medium; as, a balloon was wafted over the channel.
quotes: A gentle wafting to immortal life.
Speed the soft intercourse from soul to soul, And waft a sigh from Indus to the pole.

defs: To cause to float; to keep from sinking; to buoy.
notes: ☞ This verb is regular; but waft was formerly sometimes used, as by Shakespeare, instead of wafted.

defs: To be moved, or to pass, on a buoyant medium; to float.
quotes: And now the shouts waft near the citadel.

defs: A wave or current of wind.
quotes: In this dire season, oft the whirlwind's wing Sweeps up the burden of whole wintry plains In one wide waft.

defs: A signal made by waving something, as a flag, in the air.
A knot, or stop, in the middle of a flag.
notes: ☞ A flag with a waft in it, when hoisted at the staff, or half way to the gaff, means, a man overboard; at the peak, a desire to communicate; at the masthead, “Recall boats.”
